title: National Collegiate Athletic Association

infobox.key: [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I")  [Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II")  [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III")

infobox.value: (**NCAA**)[[b]](#cite_note-5) is a [nonprofit organization](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization") that regulates [student athletics](/wiki/College_athletics_in_the_United_States "College athletics in the United States") among about 1,100 schools in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), and [1](/wiki/Simon_Fraser_University "Simon Fraser University") in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada").[[3]](#cite_note-dotorg-4) It also organizes the [athletic](/wiki/Athletics_(physical_culture) "Athletics (physical culture)") programs of [colleges](/wiki/College "College") and helps over 500,000 college [student athletes](/wiki/Student_athlete "Student athlete") who compete annually in [college sports](/wiki/College_sports "College sports").[[3]](#cite_note-dotorg-4) The headquarters is located in [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis"), Indiana.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

North American athletic organization

"NCAA" redirects here. For the athletics association in the Philippines, see [National Collegiate Athletic Association (Philippines)](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association_(Philippines) "National Collegiate Athletic Association (Philippines)"). For other uses, see [NCAA (disambiguation)](/wiki/NCAA_(disambiguation) "NCAA (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [National Christian College Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Christian_College_Athletic_Association "National Christian College Athletic Association") or [NCCA (disambiguation)](/wiki/NCCA_(disambiguation) "NCCA (disambiguation)").

The **National Collegiate Athletic Association** (**NCAA**)[[b]](#cite_note-5) is a [nonprofit organization](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization") that regulates [student athletics](/wiki/College_athletics_in_the_United_States "College athletics in the United States") among about 1,100 schools in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), and [1](/wiki/Simon_Fraser_University "Simon Fraser University") in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada").[[3]](#cite_note-dotorg-4) It also organizes the [athletic](/wiki/Athletics_(physical_culture) "Athletics (physical culture)") programs of [colleges](/wiki/College "College") and helps over 500,000 college [student athletes](/wiki/Student_athlete "Student athlete") who compete annually in [college sports](/wiki/College_sports "College sports").[[3]](#cite_note-dotorg-4) The headquarters is located in [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis"), Indiana.

Until the 1956–57 academic year, the NCAA was a single division for all schools. That year, the NCAA split into the [University Division](/wiki/NCAA_University_Division "NCAA University Division") and the [College Division](/wiki/NCAA_College_Division "NCAA College Division").[[4]](#cite_note-:7-6) In August 1973, the current three-division system of [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I"), [Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II"), and [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") was adopted by the NCAA membership in a special convention. Under NCAA rules, Division I and Division II schools can offer [athletic scholarships](/wiki/Athletic_scholarship "Athletic scholarship") to students. Division III schools may not offer any athletic scholarships. Generally, larger schools compete in Division I and smaller schools in II and III. Division I [football](/wiki/College_football "College football") was further divided into I-A and I-AA in 1978, while Division I programs that did not have football teams were known as I-AAA. In 2006, Divisions I-A and I-AA were, respectively, renamed the [Football Bowl Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision") (FBS) and [Football Championship Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Championship Subdivision") (FCS). In its 2022–23 fiscal year, the NCAA generated $1.28 billion in revenue, $945 million (74%) of which came from airing rights to the [Division I men's basketball tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament").[[5]](#cite_note-7)

Controversially, the NCAA substantially restricts the kinds of benefits and compensation (including paid salary) that collegiate athletes could receive from their schools. The consensus among economists is these caps for men's basketball and football players benefit the athletes' schools (through [rent-seeking](/wiki/Rent-seeking "Rent-seeking")) at the expense of the athletes.[[6]](#cite_note-:5-8)[[7]](#cite_note-9)[[8]](#cite_note-10) Economists have subsequently characterized the NCAA as a [cartel](/wiki/Cartel "Cartel").[[9]](#cite_note-:3-11)[[10]](#cite_note-:4-12)[[11]](#cite_note-:6-13) In 2021, the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") unanimously ruled that some of these NCAA restrictions on student athletes are [in violation of US antitrust law](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association_v._Alston "National Collegiate Athletic Association v. Alston").[[12]](#cite_note-14) The NCAA settled [a lawsuit](/wiki/House_v._NCAA "House v. NCAA") in May 2024 allowing member institutions to pay Division I athletes who have played since 2016.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Formation and early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation and early years")]

Further information: [List of charter members of the NCAA](/wiki/List_of_charter_members_of_the_NCAA "List of charter members of the NCAA")

Intercollegiate sports began in the United States in 1852 when crews from [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") and [Yale](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") universities met in [a challenge race](/wiki/Harvard-Yale_Regatta "Harvard-Yale Regatta") in the sport of [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)").[[13]](#cite_note-15) As rowing remained the preeminent sport in the country into the late-1800s, many of the initial debates about collegiate athletic eligibility and purpose were settled through organizations like the [Rowing Association of American Colleges](/wiki/Rowing_Association_of_American_Colleges "Rowing Association of American Colleges") and the [Intercollegiate Rowing Association](/wiki/Intercollegiate_Rowing_Association "Intercollegiate Rowing Association"). As other sports emerged, notably football and basketball, many of these same concepts and standards were adopted. Football, in particular, began to emerge as a marquee sport, but the rules of the game itself were in constant flux and often had to be adapted for each contest.

The NCAA dates its formation to two White House conferences convened by President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") in the early 20th century in response to repeated injuries and deaths in college football which had "prompted many college and universities to discontinue the sport."[[1]](#cite_note-ncaahist-1) Following those White House meetings and the reforms which had resulted, Chancellor [Henry MacCracken](/wiki/Henry_MacCracken "Henry MacCracken") of [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University") organized a meeting of 13 colleges and universities to initiate changes in football playing rules; at a follow-on meeting on December 28, 1905, at the [Murray Hill Hotel](/wiki/Murray_Hill_Hotel_(New_York_City) "Murray Hill Hotel (New York City)") in New York City, 62 higher-education institutions became charter members of the **Intercollegiate Athletic Association of the United States (IAAUS)**.[[1]](#cite_note-ncaahist-1)[[14]](#cite_note-16) The IAAUS was officially established on March 31, 1906, and took its present name, the NCAA, in 1910.[[1]](#cite_note-ncaahist-1)

For several years, the NCAA was a discussion group and rules-making body, but in 1921, the first NCAA national championship was conducted: the National Collegiate Track and Field Championships.[[c]](#cite_note-18)[[16]](#cite_note-History-19) Gradually, more rules committees were formed and more championships were created, including a basketball championship in 1939.[[16]](#cite_note-History-19)

A series of crises brought the NCAA to a crossroads after World War II. The "[Sanity Code](/wiki/Sanity_Code "Sanity Code")" – adopted to establish guidelines for recruiting and financial aid – failed to curb abuses, and the Association needed to find more effective ways to curtail its membership.[[17]](#cite_note-20) Postseason football games were multiplying with little control, and member schools were increasingly concerned about how the new medium of television would affect football attendance.[[16]](#cite_note-History-19)

The NCAA engaged in a bitter power struggle with the [Amateur Athletic Union](/wiki/Amateur_Athletic_Union "Amateur Athletic Union").[[18]](#cite_note-auto10-21)[[19]](#cite_note-auto9-22) The complexity of those problems and the growth in membership and championships demonstrated the need for full-time professional leadership.

[Walter Byers](/wiki/Walter_Byers "Walter Byers"), previously an assistant sports information director, was named executive director in 1951.[[16]](#cite_note-History-19) The *[Harvard Crimson](/wiki/Harvard_Crimson "Harvard Crimson")* described Byers as "power-mad", *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* said that Byers was "secretive, despotic, stubborn and ruthless", *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* described him as a dictator, and others described him as a "petty tyrant".[[18]](#cite_note-auto10-21)[[20]](#cite_note-23)[[21]](#cite_note-24)[[22]](#cite_note-25)[[23]](#cite_note-auto3-26)[[24]](#cite_note-27)[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[25]](#cite_note-28)

Byers wasted no time placing his stamp on the Association, and a national headquarters was established in [Kansas City, Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"), in 1952.[[16]](#cite_note-History-19) A program to control live television of football games was approved, the annual Convention delegated enforcement powers to the Association's Council, and legislation was adopted governing postseason bowl games.[[16]](#cite_note-History-19)

### 1970s–1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1970s–1980s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/NCAA_70s_logo.svg/250px-NCAA_70s_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:NCAA_70s_logo.svg)

The NCAA logo used from 1971 to 1979

As [college athletics](/wiki/College_athletics "College athletics") grew, the scope of the nation's athletics programs diverged, forcing the NCAA to create a structure that recognized varying levels of emphasis. In 1973, the association's membership was divided into three legislative and competitive divisions – I, II, and III.[[26]](#cite_note-29) Five years later in 1978, Division I members voted to create subdivisions I-A and I-AA (renamed the Football Bowl Subdivision and the Football Championship Subdivision in 2006) in football.[[16]](#cite_note-History-19)

Until the 1980s, the association did not govern women's athletics. Instead, the [Association for Intercollegiate Athletics for Women](/wiki/Association_for_Intercollegiate_Athletics_for_Women "Association for Intercollegiate Athletics for Women") (AIAW), with nearly 1,000 member schools, governed women's collegiate sports in the United States. The AIAW was in a vulnerable position that precipitated conflicts with the NCAA in the early-1980s. Following a one-year overlap in which both organizations staged women's championships, the AIAW discontinued operation, and most member schools continued their women's athletics programs under the governance of the NCAA.[[27]](#cite_note-30) By 1982 all divisions of the NCAA offered national championship events for women's athletics. A year later in 1983, the 75th Convention approved an expansion to plan women's athletic program services and pushed for a women's championship program.[[16]](#cite_note-History-19)

### President's Commission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: President's Commission")]

Proposals at every NCAA Convention are voted on by the institutional members of the NCAA. Each institutional member has one representative: the president/CEO or a representative designated by him/her.[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)
Attendance by the actual president/CEO was low; less than 30%.[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31) [Southern Methodist University](/wiki/Southern_Methodist_University "Southern Methodist University") President [A. Kenneth Pye](/wiki/A._Kenneth_Pye "A. Kenneth Pye") commented, "In too many cases, presidents have not only delegated responsibility, they have abdicated it."[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31) Many presidents designated their athletic director as the institutional representative, something Pye compared to "entrusting a chicken coop to the supervision of a wolf and a fox."[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)
Beginning around 1980, a group of college presidents thought there was a crisis of integrity in collegiate sports and discussed ways to
transform athletics to match the academic model. The [American Council on Education](/wiki/American_Council_on_Education "American Council on Education") (ACE) proposed a presidential board empowered to veto NCAA membership actions, while the NCAA Council, whose membership was mostly athletic officials, suggested a presidential commission with advisory powers. The council's proposal may have been intended to block the presidential effort to gain control of the NCAA. The two proposals were voted on by the membership at the NCAA Convention in January 1984. The ACE proposal was defeated by a vote of 313 to 328. The Council proposal passed on a voice vote without ballots.[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)
Publicly, the *President's Commission* **(PC)** was responsible for establishing an agenda for the NCAA, but the actual language of the proposal stated that their role was to be a presidential forum and to provide the NCAA with the president's position on major policy issues. The PC could study issues and urge action, call special meetings and sponsor legislation. Their one real power was to veto the selection of Executive Director.[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)[[29]](#cite_note-32) The composition of the commission was 22 CEOs from Division I and 11 CEOs each from Divisions II and III.[[30]](#cite_note-UPI-33)
The true intent of the PC was to shift control of intercollegiate athletics back to CEOs. Graduation rates were an important metric to chancellors and presidents and became a focus of the PC.[[31]](#cite_note-34)[[32]](#cite_note-35)

In June 1985 a special convention was held to review legislative proposals including academic integrity, academic-reporting requirements, differences in "major" and "secondary" violations including the "death penalty" and requiring an annual financial audit of athletic departments. All proposals passed overwhelmingly. Many presidents who did not attend sent a vice-president rather than their athletic director.[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31) [University of Florida](/wiki/University_of_Florida "University of Florida") President [Marshall Criser](/wiki/Marshall_Criser "Marshall Criser") stated that "the ultimate responsibility must be assumed by the CEOs because we don't have enough NCAA cops to solve all of the problems."[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)

The regular NCAA meeting in January 1986 presented proposals in regard to college eligibility, drug testing, and basketball competition limits.[[33]](#cite_note-36) All passed but matters regarding acceptable academic progress, special-admissions and booster club activities were ignored. Many presidents did not attend and it appeared that athletic directors controlled the meeting. A survey of 138 Division I presidents indicated that athletic directors did control collegiate sports. Despite a moratorium on extending the season of any sport in 1985, the extension of basketball and hockey seasons were approved. [Indiana University](/wiki/Indiana_University "Indiana University") president [John W. Ryan](/wiki/John_W._Ryan "John W. Ryan"), outgoing chairman of the PC commented, "If the moratorium is vacated, it's being vacated not by the commission, but by this convention."[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31) Following the vote, a delegate was quoted, "A lot of Athletic Directors figure they've successfully waited out the presidents...unless the presidents fight back, NCAA reform is flat-ass dead in the water."[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)

The PC proposed just one legislative issue at the January 1987 meeting: applying the minimum academic standards in Division I to Division II. It narrowly passed.[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)

The PC attempted to again push the reform of college athletics by calling another special convention which was held in June 1987 to discuss cost-cutting measures and to address the overemphasis on athletics in colleges and universities. John Slaughter, [Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor "Chancellor") of the [University of Maryland](/wiki/University_of_Maryland,_College_Park "University of Maryland, College Park") served as chairman. He stated, "This represents the second major thrust since our commission was formed three years ago. The first involved academics and infractions. This will be equally momentous and more sweeping. We want to achieve a balance between athletics and other institutional programs."[[30]](#cite_note-UPI-33)
Cost-cutting measures proposed included reductions in athletic financial aid, coaching staff sizes, and length of practice/playing seasons. A resolution was also floated that opposed coaches receiving outside financial compensation if outside activities interfere with regular duties.[[30]](#cite_note-UPI-33) All the PC proposals were defeated, and two basketball scholarships were restored that were eliminated at the meeting in January. It was apparent that there was an open conflict between college presidents.[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)
The president of the [Carnegie Foundation for the Advancement of Teaching](/wiki/Carnegie_Foundation_for_the_Advancement_of_Teaching "Carnegie Foundation for the Advancement of Teaching"), [Ernest L. Boyer](/wiki/Ernest_L._Boyer "Ernest L. Boyer"), summarized the situation: "There are presidents whose institutions are so deeply involved in athletics that their own institutional and personal futures hang in the balance. They feel they must resist such change because athletics are bigger than they are."[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)

The PC sponsored no legislation at the January 1988 annual meeting, and there was not a vote of confidence.[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)

However, a year later at the annual meeting, financial aid restrictions were proposed for specific Division I and II sports. Following extensive discussions, the measure was withdrawn and a *Special Committee on Cost Reductions* was formed to study the issue. Once again, a proposal from the PC was circumvented.[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)

The President's Commission met in October 1989 to prepare for the 1990 NCAA annual meeting. Proposals were developed to shorten spring football and the basketball season; grant financial aid based on need to academically deficient athletes; and reporting of graduation rates. Chancellor [Martin Massengale](/wiki/Martin_Massengale "Martin Massengale") of the [University of Nebraska–Lincoln](/wiki/University_of_Nebraska%E2%80%93Lincoln "University of Nebraska–Lincoln") was then chairman of the PC insisted that graduation rate data was needed to preclude "further need for federal legislation" that was being proposed by Representative [Tom McMillen](/wiki/Tom_McMillen "Tom McMillen") and Senator [Bill Bradley](/wiki/Bill_Bradley "Bill Bradley") (both former NCAA and NBA players).[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31) The proposals demonstrated that the PC was intent on regaining control of college athletics and the opposition was immediate. Commissioner of the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference") [Jim Delany](/wiki/Jim_Delany "Jim Delany") responded, "They tend to want quick answers and you don't solve the complexities of intercollegiate athletics. Yes, presidents are involved, but the truth is, they really don't have time to be involved."[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31) [Bo Schembechler](/wiki/Bo_Schembechler "Bo Schembechler"), then [University of Michigan](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines "Michigan Wolverines") head football coach and athletic director, was blunt, saying "Unfortunately, you're dealing with people who don't understand. We're trying to straddle the fence here because you still want me to put 100,000 (fans) in the stadium and the reason you want me to do it is because you're not going to help me financially at all."[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31) In 1990, he resigned his college jobs to become president of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball")'s [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers"). Upon his departure, he predicted, "In the next five years, school presidents will completely confuse intercollegiate athletics directors, then they'll dump it back to athletics directors and say, 'You straighten this out.' About 2000, it may be back on track."[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)

Presidential turnout for the January 1990 meeting was good and many who did not attend sent a delegate to vote for the PC. The graduation reporting proposal passed overwhelmingly, and the proposal for need-based non-athletic aid passed easily. The final proposal to shorten basketball and spring football generated fierce debate. There was a motion to defer the proposal for study that failed 383–363, but the many PC members relaxed, confident of victory. PC Chairman Massengale left the meeting for other business, but during lunch, council members began lobbying and twisting arms to change votes. When the session resumed, council members began criticizing the PC and quickly executed a parliamentary maneuver to refer the proposal to the NCAA Council. Many PC members were still at lunch when a roll call vote passed 170–150. [University of Texas](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns "Texas Longhorns") women's athletic director [Donna Lopiano](/wiki/Donna_Lopiano "Donna Lopiano") complained, "The President's Commission needs to do what it does best, and that is to macro-manage. Leave the micro-management to the various expert groups. We
will bring back solutions."[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)
Numerous presidents were shocked, upset and angry, but the remaining PC members began their own lobbying and arm-twisting. An hour later, there was a sense that representatives who had voted against the direction of their respective presidents had reconsidered, and a motion was made to reconsider by [Lattie F. Coor](/wiki/Lattie_F._Coor "Lattie F. Coor"), president of [Arizona State University](/wiki/Arizona_State_University "Arizona State University"). [West Point](/wiki/United_States_Military_Academy "United States Military Academy") Lieutenant General [Dave Richard Palmer](/wiki/Dave_Richard_Palmer "Dave Richard Palmer") urged the vote, stating the NCAA needed "to make a mark on the wall...delay is the deadliest form of denial."
[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31) Following discussion, compromise and voting on minor issues, the reconsideration motion passed, and the third proposal was adopted with a vote of 165–156.[[28]](#cite_note-BARK-31)

The President's Commission held hearings beginning on May 9, 1991, to develop stronger academic standards.[[34]](#cite_note-37)

The President's Commission lasted for 13 years and pushed through initiatives such as restricting the size of coaching staffs; limiting how much time student-athletes can spend on their sports; and setting more demanding academic standards for Divisions I and II.[[35]](#cite_note-38)
By the 1980s, [televised college football](/wiki/College_football_on_television "College football on television") had become a larger source of income for the NCAA. In September 1981, the Board of Regents of the [University of Oklahoma](/wiki/University_of_Oklahoma "University of Oklahoma") and the [University of Georgia](/wiki/University_of_Georgia "University of Georgia") Athletic Association filed suit against the NCAA in [district court](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Western_District_of_Oklahoma "United States District Court for the Western District of Oklahoma") in [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma"). The plaintiffs stated that the NCAA's football television plan constituted price fixing, output restraints, boycott, and monopolizing, all of which were illegal under the [Sherman Act](/wiki/Sherman_Act "Sherman Act"). The NCAA argued that its pro-competitive and non-commercial justifications for the plan – protection of live gate, maintenance of competitive balance among NCAA member institutions, and the creation of a more attractive "product" to compete with other 7forms of entertainment – combined to make the plan reasonable. In September 1982, the district court found in favor of the plaintiffs, ruling that the plan violated antitrust laws. It enjoined the association from enforcing the contract. The NCAA appealed all the way to the [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States"), but lost in 1984 in a 7–2 ruling *[NCAA v. Board of Regents of the University of Oklahoma](/wiki/NCAA_v._Board_of_Regents_of_the_University_of_Oklahoma "NCAA v. Board of Regents of the University of Oklahoma")*.[[36]](#cite_note-NCAA_v._Oklahoma-39) (If the television contracts the NCAA had with [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"), [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), and [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") had remained in effect for the 1984 season, they would have generated some $73.6 million for the association and its members.)

### Late 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Late 1990s")]

In 1999, the NCAA was sued for discriminating against female athletes under [Title IX](/wiki/Title_IX "Title IX") for systematically giving men in graduate school more waivers than a woman to participate in college sports. In *[National Collegiate Athletic Association v. Smith](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association_v._Smith "National Collegiate Athletic Association v. Smith")*, 525 U.S. 459 (1999) the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the NCAA was not subject to that law, without reviewing the merits of the discrimination claim.[[37]](#cite_note-NCAA_v._Smith-40)

Over the last two decades recruiting international athletes has become a growing trend among NCAA institutions. For example, most German athletes outside of Germany are based at US universities. For many European athletes, the American universities are the only option to pursue an academic and athletic career at the same time. Many of these students come to the US with high academic expectations and aspirations.[[38]](#cite_note-41)

In 2009, [Simon Fraser University](/wiki/Simon_Fraser_University "Simon Fraser University") in [Burnaby](/wiki/Burnaby "Burnaby"), [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), Canada, became the NCAA's first non-US member institution, joining Division II.[[39]](#cite_note-42)[[40]](#cite_note-43) In 2018, Division II membership approved allowing schools from [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") to apply for membership; [CETYS](/wiki/Centro_de_Ense%C3%B1anza_T%C3%A9cnica_y_Superior "Centro de Enseñanza Técnica y Superior") of [Tijuana](/wiki/Tijuana "Tijuana"), [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California") expressed significant interest in joining at the time.[[41]](#cite_note-44)[[42]](#cite_note-45) However, Simon Fraser announced in 2025 that it had applied to return to Canada's national governing body of [U Sports](/wiki/U_Sports "U Sports"), and would leave the NCAA in 2027 if its application was accepted.[[43]](#cite_note-46)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/SFU_Men%27s_Soccer_vs_Northwest_Nazarene.jpg/250px-SFU_Men%27s_Soccer_vs_Northwest_Nazarene.jpg)](/wiki/File:SFU_Men%27s_Soccer_vs_Northwest_Nazarene.jpg)

A men's soccer match between Canada's only NCAA school, [Simon Fraser University](/wiki/Simon_Fraser_Red_Leafs "Simon Fraser Red Leafs"), and Idaho's [Northwest Nazarene University](/wiki/Northwest_Nazarene_Nighthawks "Northwest Nazarene Nighthawks") in 2012

In 2014, the NCAA set a record high of $989 million in net revenue. Just shy of $1 billion, it is among the highest of all large sports organizations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During the NCAA's 2022 annual convention, the membership ratified a new version of the organization's constitution. The new constitution dramatically simplifies a rulebook that many college sports leaders saw as increasingly bloated.

It also reduced the size of the NCAA Board of Governors from 20 to 9, and guaranteed that current and former athletes have voting representation on both the NCAA board and the governing bodies of each NCAA division. The new constitution was the first step in a reorganization process in which each division will have the right to set its own rules, with no approval needed from the rest of the NCAA membership.[[44]](#cite_note-47)[[45]](#cite_note-48)

### Notable court cases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Notable court cases")]

* In the late-1940s, there were only two colleges in the country, [Notre Dame](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_football "Notre Dame Fighting Irish football") and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Penn_Quakers_football "Penn Quakers football"), with national TV contracts, a considerable source of revenue. In 1951, the NCAA voted to prohibit any live TV broadcast of college football games during the season. No sooner had the NCAA voted to ban television than public outcry forced it to retreat. Instead, the NCAA voted to restrict the number of televised games for each team to stop the slide in gate attendance. [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania") president [Harold Stassen](/wiki/Harold_Stassen "Harold Stassen") defied the monopoly and renewed its contract with [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"). Eventually, Penn dropped its suit when the NCAA, refusing Penn's request that the U.S. Attorney General rule on the legality of the NCAA's restrictive plan,[[46]](#cite_note-49) threatened to expel the university from the association. Notre Dame continued televising its games through 1953, working around the ban by filming its games, then broadcasting them the next evening.[[47]](#cite_note-50)
* In 1957, the Colorado Supreme Court dismissed a lawsuit filed by the family of deceased Trinidad College football player Ray Herbert Dennison. Despite suffering a lethal concussion injury on the field in a game versus Fort Lewis A&M College, Dennison was not entitled to any compensation because he was not under a contractual obligation to play football. Furthermore, the court stated that the "college did not receive a direct benefit from the activities, since the college was not in the football business and received no benefit from this field of recreation".[[48]](#cite_note-51)
* In 1977, prompted partly by the Tarkanian Case, the US Congress initiated an investigation into the NCAA.[[49]](#cite_note-52) It, combined with Tarkanian's case, forced the NCAA's internal files into the public record.[[50]](#cite_note-53)
* In 1984, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in *[NCAA v. Board of Regents of the University of Oklahoma](/wiki/NCAA_v._Board_of_Regents_of_the_University_of_Oklahoma "NCAA v. Board of Regents of the University of Oklahoma")* that the NCAA's complete control of television rights violated the [Sherman](/wiki/Sherman_Antitrust_Act_of_1890 "Sherman Antitrust Act of 1890") and [Clayton Antitrust Acts](/wiki/Clayton_Antitrust_Act_of_1914 "Clayton Antitrust Act of 1914"), paving the way for schools and conferences to independently make deals with TV broadcasters directly.[[36]](#cite_note-NCAA_v._Oklahoma-39)[[51]](#cite_note-54)
* In 1998, the NCAA settled a $2.5 million lawsuit filed by former UNLV basketball coach, [Jerry Tarkanian](/wiki/Jerry_Tarkanian "Jerry Tarkanian"). Tarkanian sued the NCAA after he was forced to resign from UNLV, where he had been head coach from 1973 to 1992. The suit claimed the agency singled him out, penalizing the university's basketball program three times in that span. Tarkanian said, "They can never, ever, make up for all the pain and agony they caused me. All I can say is that for 25 years they beat the hell out of me". The NCAA said that it regretted the long battle and it now has more understanding of Tarkanian's position and that the case has changed the enforcement process for the better.[[52]](#cite_note-55)
* In 1999, the NCAA was sued for discriminating against female athletes under [Title IX](/wiki/Title_IX "Title IX") for systematically giving men in graduate school more waivers than women to participate in college sports. In *[National Collegiate Athletic Association v. Smith](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association_v._Smith "National Collegiate Athletic Association v. Smith")*, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the NCAA was not subject to that law, without reviewing the merits of the discrimination claim.[[37]](#cite_note-NCAA_v._Smith-40)[[53]](#cite_note-56)
* In 2007, the case of *White et al. v. NCAA, No. CV 06-999-RGK* (C.D. Cal. September 20, 2006) was brought by former NCAA student-athletes [Jason White](/wiki/Jason_White_(American_football) "Jason White (American football)"), Brian Pollack, Jovan Harris, and Chris Craig as a class action lawsuit. They argued that the NCAA's current limits on a full scholarship or grant-in-aid was a violation of federal antitrust laws. Their reasoning was that in the absence of such a limit, NCAA member schools would be free to offer any financial aid packages they desired to recruit student athletes. The NCAA settled before a ruling by the court, by agreeing to set up the Former Student-Athlete Fund to "assist qualified candidates applying for receipt of career development expenses and/or reimbursement of educational expenses under the terms of the agreement with plaintiffs in a federal antitrust lawsuit."[[54]](#cite_note-57)
* In 2013, [Jay Bilas](/wiki/Jay_Bilas "Jay Bilas") claimed that the NCAA was taking advantage of individual players through jersey sales in its store. Specifically, he typed the names of several top college football players, [Tajh Boyd](/wiki/Tajh_Boyd "Tajh Boyd"), [Teddy Bridgewater](/wiki/Teddy_Bridgewater "Teddy Bridgewater"), [Jadeveon Clowney](/wiki/Jadeveon_Clowney "Jadeveon Clowney"), [Johnny Manziel](/wiki/Johnny_Manziel "Johnny Manziel"), and [A. J. McCarron](/wiki/A._J._McCarron "A. J. McCarron"), into the search engine of the NCAA's official online store. The search results returned corresponding numbered team jerseys. The NCAA subsequently removed the team jerseys listed on its site.[[55]](#cite_note-Swaim-58)
* In March 2014, four players filed a class action [antitrust](/wiki/Antitrust "Antitrust") lawsuit (*[O'Bannon v. NCAA](/wiki/O%27Bannon_v._NCAA "O'Bannon v. NCAA")*), alleging that the NCAA and its five dominant conferences are an "unlawful cartel". The suit charges that NCAA caps on the value of athletic scholarships have "illegally restricted the earning power of football and men's basketball players while making billions off their labor".[[56]](#cite_note-59) Tulane University Sports Law Program Director Gabe Feldman called the suit "an instantly credible threat to the NCAA". On September 30, 2015, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit ruled that limiting compensation to the cost of an athlete's attendance at a university was sufficient. It simultaneously ruled against a federal judge's proposal to pay student athletes $5,000 per year in deferred compensation.[[57]](#cite_note-60)
* In August 2015, the National Labor Relations Board reversed a decision settled in the prior year that classified members of Northwestern University's scholarship football players as employees, thus, granting them the right to collectively bargain for their rights. The unionization efforts were a direct effort led by the College Athletes Player Association and Kain Colter, who operated with the support of the United Steelworkers group.[[58]](#cite_note-:2-61) The case was ultimately struck down due to difficulties in applying the ruling across both public and private institutions. The NCAA made several improvements to the value of athletic scholarships and the quality of healthcare coverage in response to this movement by the Northwestern football players.[[58]](#cite_note-:2-61) These reforms included guaranteeing the entire four years of scholarship in the event of a career-ending injury, the implementation of "cost of attendance" stipends, the institution of "unlimited" athlete meal plans, and protections for the name, image, and likeness of athletes by third parties such as Electronic Arts.[[58]](#cite_note-:2-61)
* In 2018, former [UCF](/wiki/University_of_Central_Florida "University of Central Florida") kicker [Donald De La Haye](/wiki/Donald_De_La_Haye "Donald De La Haye") filed a lawsuit alleging that the university violated his First Amendment rights when it rescinded his full athletic scholarship over the income De La Haye made from his monetized YouTube channel, which he started before he attended college. [UCF](/wiki/University_of_Central_Florida "University of Central Florida") argued De La Haye violated the [NCAA](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") policy forbidding student-athletes from using their likenesses to make money.[[59]](#cite_note-62) De La Haye ultimately settled with [UCF](/wiki/University_of_Central_Florida "University of Central Florida") so that he could obtain his degree from the university.
* In June 2021, the Supreme Court of the United States unanimously affirmed a ruling in [*NCAA v. Alston*](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association_v._Alston "National Collegiate Athletic Association v. Alston") that provides for an incremental increase in how college athletes can be compensated. Justice Neil Gorsuch wrote the court's opinion, which upheld a district court judge's decision that the NCAA was violating antitrust law by placing limits on the education-related benefits that schools can provide to athletes. The decision allows schools to provide their athletes with unlimited compensation as long as it is some way connected to their education. The idea that college athletes should not be paid, a fundamental tenet of the 115-year-old NCAA, has faced increasing scrutiny in recent years. Federal antitrust lawsuits have slowly eroded strict [amateurism](/wiki/Amateur_sports "Amateur sports") rules during the past decade.[[60]](#cite_note-63)

## Headquarters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Headquarters")]

The modern era of the NCAA began in July 1955 when its executive director, [Kansas City, Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri") native [Walter Byers](/wiki/Walter_Byers "Walter Byers"), moved the organization's headquarters from the LaSalle Hotel in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") (where its offices were shared by the headquarters of the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference")) to the Fairfax Building in [Downtown Kansas City](/wiki/Downtown_Kansas_City "Downtown Kansas City"). The move was intended to separate the NCAA from the direct influence of any individual conference and keep it centrally located.

The Fairfax was a block from [Municipal Auditorium](/wiki/Municipal_Auditorium_(Kansas_City) "Municipal Auditorium (Kansas City)") which had hosted men's basketball Final Four games in 1940, 1941, and 1942. After Byers moved the headquarters to Kansas City, the championships would be held in Municipal Auditorium in 1953, 1954, 1955, 1957, 1961, and 1964. The Fairfax office consisted of three rooms with no air conditioning. Byers' staff consisted of four people: an assistant, two secretaries, and a bookkeeper.[[61]](#cite_note-64)

In 1964, the NCAA moved three blocks away to offices in the [Midland Theatre](/wiki/Midland_Theatre "Midland Theatre"), moving again in 1973 to a $1.2 million building on 3.4 acres (14,000 m2) on [Shawnee Mission Parkway](/wiki/Shawnee_Mission_Parkway "Shawnee Mission Parkway") in suburban [Mission, Kansas](/wiki/Mission,_Kansas "Mission, Kansas"). In 1989, the organization moved 6 miles (9.7 km) farther south to [Overland Park, Kansas](/wiki/Overland_Park,_Kansas "Overland Park, Kansas"). The new building was on 11.35 acres (45,900 m2) and had 130,000 square feet (12,000 m2) of space.[[62]](#cite_note-65)

The NCAA was dissatisfied with its [Johnson County, Kansas](/wiki/Johnson_County,_Kansas "Johnson County, Kansas") suburban location, noting that its location on the southern edges of the Kansas City suburbs was more than 40 minutes from [Kansas City International Airport](/wiki/Kansas_City_International_Airport "Kansas City International Airport"). They also noted that the suburban location was not drawing visitors to its new visitors' center.[[63]](#cite_note-allbusiness.com-66)

In 1997, it asked for bids for a new headquarters. Various cities competed for a new headquarters with the two finalists being Kansas City and Indianapolis. Kansas City proposed to relocate the NCAA back downtown near the [Crown Center](/wiki/Crown_Center "Crown Center") complex and would locate the visitors' center in [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(Kansas_City,_Missouri) "Union Station (Kansas City, Missouri)"). However, Kansas City's main sports venue at the time, [Kemper Arena](/wiki/Hy-Vee_Arena "Hy-Vee Arena"), was nearly 23 years old.[[63]](#cite_note-allbusiness.com-66) Indianapolis argued that it was in fact more central than Kansas City in that two-thirds of the members are east of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River").[[63]](#cite_note-allbusiness.com-66) The 50,000-seat [RCA Dome](/wiki/RCA_Dome "RCA Dome") (since replaced by the larger [Lucas Oil Stadium](/wiki/Lucas_Oil_Stadium "Lucas Oil Stadium")) far eclipsed 19,500-seat Kemper Arena. In 1999, the NCAA moved its 300-member staff to its new headquarters in the [White River State Park](/wiki/White_River_State_Park "White River State Park") in a four-story 140,000-square-foot (13,000 m2) facility on the west edge of downtown [Indianapolis, Indiana](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis"). Adjacent to the headquarters is the 35,000-square-foot (3,300 m2) [NCAA Hall of Champions](/wiki/NCAA_Hall_of_Champions "NCAA Hall of Champions").[[64]](#cite_note-67)

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Structure")]

The NCAA's Board of Governors (formerly known as the Executive Committee) is the main body within the NCAA. This body elects the NCAA's president.[[65]](#cite_note-68)

The NCAA's legislative structure is broken down into cabinets and committees, consisting of various representatives of its member schools.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] These may be broken down further into sub-committees. The legislation is then passed on to the Management Council, which oversees all the cabinets and committees, and also includes representatives from the schools, such as athletic directors and faculty advisers. Management Council legislation goes on to the Board of Directors, which consists of school presidents, for final approval. The NCAA national office staff provides support by acting as guides, liaisons, researchers, and by managing public and media relations.

The NCAA runs the officiating software company [ArbiterSports](/wiki/ArbiterSports "ArbiterSports"), based in [Sandy, Utah](/wiki/Sandy,_Utah "Sandy, Utah"), a joint venture between two subsidiaries of the NCAA, Arbiter LLC and eOfficials LLC. The NCAA's stated objective for the venture is to help improve the fairness, quality, and consistency of officiating across amateur athletics.[[66]](#cite_note-69)[[67]](#cite_note-70)

### Division history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Division history")]

Further information: [List of NCAA Division I institutions](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_Division_I_institutions "List of NCAA Division I institutions"), [List of NCAA Division II institutions](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_Division_II_institutions "List of NCAA Division II institutions"), and [List of NCAA Division III institutions](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_Division_III_institutions "List of NCAA Division III institutions")

Before 1957, all NCAA sports used a single division of competition. In 1957 the NCAA split into two divisions for men's basketball only, with major programs making up the [University Division](/wiki/NCAA_University_Division "NCAA University Division") and smaller programs making up the [College Division](/wiki/NCAA_College_Division "NCAA College Division").[[4]](#cite_note-:7-6) The names could be confusing, as some schools with "University" in their name still competed in the College Division while some with "College" in their name competed in the University Division. The split gradually took hold in other sports as well. Records from before the split were inherited by the University Division.

In 1973 the College Division split up between teams that wanted to grant athletic scholarships (becoming Division II, which inherited the College Division's records and history) and teams that did not (becoming Division III), and the University Division was renamed to Division I. Division I split into two subdivisions for football only in 1978 (though both still under the Division I name), with Division I-A consisting of major teams who would continue to compete in bowl games and use various polls to decide its champion and Division I-AA consisting of smaller teams who would compete in the new NCAA Football Tournament to decide its champion.[[73]](#cite_note-bswb-76) Division I schools without football teams were known as Division I-AAA. In 2006, Division I-A became the Football Bowl Subdivision (FBS), Division I-AA became the Football Championship Subdivision (FCS), and Division I-AAA became Division I non-football. The changes were in name only with no significant structural differences to the organization.

#### "National Collegiate" sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: \"National Collegiate\" sports")]

For some less-popular sports, the NCAA does not separate teams into their usual divisions and instead holds only one tournament to decide a single national champion between all three divisions (except for women's ice hockey and men's indoor volleyball, where the National Collegiate championship only features teams from Division I and Division II and a separate championship is contested for only Division III). The 13 sports which use the National Collegiate format, also called the single-division format, are women's bowling, men's fencing, women's fencing, men's gymnastics, women's gymnastics, women's ice hockey, rifle, skiing, men's indoor volleyball, women's beach volleyball, men's water polo, women's water polo, and women's wrestling.[[74]](#cite_note-77) The newest such sport is women's wrestling, which held its first NCAA championship in the 2025–26 school year.[[75]](#cite_note-WomensWrestlingAdded-78) The all-female sports of acrobatics & tumbling and stunt, the latter a cheerleading discipline, will hold their first NCAA championships using this format in 2026–27.[[76]](#cite_note-4NewChamps-79)

The NCAA considers a National Collegiate title equivalent to a Division I title even if the champion is primarily a member of Division II or III.[[77]](#cite_note-fs.ncaa.org-80) These championships are largely dominated by teams that are otherwise members of Division I, but current non-Division I teams have won 41 National Collegiate championships since the University Division/College Division split as of March 2026 (2 in bowling, 20 in fencing, 8 in women's ice hockey, 10 in rifle, and 1 in women's wrestling).[[77]](#cite_note-fs.ncaa.org-80) Division III schools are allowed to grant athletic scholarships to students who compete in National Collegiate sports, though most do not.

Men's ice hockey uses a similar but not identical "National Collegiate" format as women's ice hockey and men's indoor volleyball (Division III has its own championship but several Division III teams compete in Division I for men's ice hockey), but its top-level championship is branded as a "Division I" championship. While the NCAA has not explained why it is the only sport with this distinction, the NCAA held a separate [Division II championship](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II_men%27s_ice_hockey_tournament "NCAA Division II men's ice hockey tournament") from 1978 to 1984 and again from 1993 to 1999. As of [2025](/wiki/2025_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_ice_hockey_tournament "2025 NCAA Division I men's ice hockey tournament"), 12 Division I men's ice hockey championships have been won by current non-Division I teams since the University Division/College Division split. Like with National Collegiate sports, schools that are otherwise members of Division III who compete in Division I for men's ice hockey are allowed to grant athletic scholarships for the sport.

All sports used the National Collegiate format until 1957, when the NCAA was split into the [University Division](/wiki/NCAA_University_Division "NCAA University Division") and [College Division](/wiki/NCAA_College_Division "NCAA College Division") (which itself was split into Divisions II and III in 1973).[[4]](#cite_note-:7-6) The only sport that immediately saw a change after the 1957 split was men's basketball;[[d]](#cite_note-81) all other sports continued to use the National Collegiate format for at least one season, and usually many more. Some sports that began after the split once used the format and no longer do. This include men's and women's lacrosse, women's rowing, women's soccer, and men's and women's indoor track & field.

Some sports, including men's and women's golf, men's ice hockey, men's lacrosse, and men's and women's soccer used to have a combined championship between Divisions II and III, but these were known as a "Division II/III championship" in most cases. The NCAA considered these titles equivalent to a Division II title.[[77]](#cite_note-fs.ncaa.org-80) No sport currently uses this format.

## Player eligibility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Player eligibility")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article's **[factual accuracy](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") may be compromised due to out-of-date information**. Relevant discussion may be found on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association#Former_pros_playing_in_college "Talk:National Collegiate Athletic Association"). Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(January 2026)* |

See also: [Amateurism in the NCAA](/wiki/Amateurism_in_the_NCAA "Amateurism in the NCAA")

The NCAA requires all of its athletes to be [amateurs](/wiki/Amateur_sports "Amateur sports"). All incoming athletes must be certified as amateurs. To remain eligible, athletes must not sign contract with sports clubs, earn a salary playing a sport, try out for professional sports, or enter into agreements with [agents](/wiki/Sports_agent "Sports agent").[[78]](#cite_note-82)

To participate in college athletics in their freshman year, the NCAA requires that students meet three criteria: having graduated from high school, be completing the minimum required academic courses, and having qualifying grade-point average (GPA).[[79]](#cite_note-83)

The 16 academic credits are four courses in English, two courses in math, two classes in social science, two in natural or physical science, and one additional course in English, math, natural or physical science, or another academic course such as a foreign language.[[80]](#cite_note-84)

To meet the Division I requirements for grade point average, the lowest possible high school GPA a student may have to be eligible with to play in their freshman year is a 2.30 (2.20 for Division II or III), but they are allowed to play beginning in their second year with a GPA of 2.00.[[81]](#cite_note-hishinuma-85)

As of the 2017–18 school year, a high school student may sign a [letter of intent](/wiki/National_Letter_of_Intent "National Letter of Intent") to enter and play football for a Division I or Division II college in either of two periods.[[e]](#cite_note-87) The first, introduced in 2017–18, is a three-day period in mid-December, coinciding with the first three days of the previously existing signing period for junior college players.[[83]](#cite_note-88) The second period, which before 2017 was the only one allowed for signings of high school players, starts on the first Wednesday in February.[[84]](#cite_note-89) In August 2011, the NCAA announced plans to raise academic requirements for postseason competition, including its two most prominent competitions, football's now-defunct [Bowl Championship Series](/wiki/Bowl_Championship_Series "Bowl Championship Series") (replaced in 2014 by the [College Football Playoff](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff "College Football Playoff")) and the [Division I men's basketball tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"); the new requirement, which are based on an "[Academic Progress Rate](/wiki/Academic_Progress_Rate "Academic Progress Rate")" (APR) that measures retention and graduation rates, and is calculated on a four-year, rolling basis.[[85]](#cite_note-cnn930rate-90) The changes raise the rate from 900 to 930, which represents a 50% graduation rate.[[85]](#cite_note-cnn930rate-90)

Student-athletes can accept prize money from tournaments or competitions if they do not exceed the total expenses from the event. For example, during high school, D1 tennis players may take up to $10,000 in total prize money. If the student surpassed the amount of $10,000 of prize money in a calendar year, they would lose eligibility.[[86]](#cite_note-91)

Students are only allowed to compete athletically for four years. Athletes are allowed to sit out a year while still attending school but not lose a year of eligibility by [redshirting](/wiki/Redshirt_(college_sports) "Redshirt (college sports)"). In other words, a student has five years from the time they begin college to play four seasons before getting drafted to any professional sports teams.

## NCAA sponsored sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: NCAA sponsored sports")]

The NCAA currently awards 92 national team championships yearly – 47 women's, 43 men's, and 2 coed championships for rifle and skiing. Sports sanctioned by the NCAA include the following: [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") (men), [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field"), [softball](/wiki/Softball "Softball") (women), [football](/wiki/College_football "College football") (men), [cross country](/wiki/Cross_country_running "Cross country running"), [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") (women), [bowling](/wiki/Ten-pin_bowling "Ten-pin bowling") (women), [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf"), [fencing](/wiki/Fencing_(sport) "Fencing (sport)"), [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse"), [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [gymnastics](/wiki/Artistic_gymnastics "Artistic gymnastics"), [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)") (women), [swimming](/wiki/Swimming_(sport) "Swimming (sport)") and [diving](/wiki/Diving_(sport) "Diving (sport)"), [beach volleyball](/wiki/Beach_volleyball "Beach volleyball") (women), volleyball, [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"), [water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo"), [rifle](/wiki/Shooting_sports "Shooting sports") (coeducational), [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis"), [skiing](/wiki/Alpine_skiing "Alpine skiing") (coeducational), and [wrestling](/wiki/Collegiate_wrestling "Collegiate wrestling").

The newest sports to be officially sanctioned are [acrobatics](/wiki/Acrobatic_gymnastics "Acrobatic gymnastics") & [tumbling](/wiki/Tumbling_(sport) "Tumbling (sport)") and the cheerleading discipline of stunt, all-female sports which will hold their first official championships in spring 2027.[[76]](#cite_note-4NewChamps-79) Before then, the last sport to have been officially sanctioned was women's wrestling, which held its first championship in March 2026,[[75]](#cite_note-WomensWrestlingAdded-78) preceded by beach volleyball, which held its [first championship](/wiki/2016_NCAA_Beach_Volleyball_Championship "2016 NCAA Beach Volleyball Championship") in spring 2016.[[87]](#cite_note-92) The NCAA had called that sport "sand volleyball" until June 23, 2015, when it announced that it would use the internationally recognized name of "beach volleyball".[[88]](#cite_note-93)

The Division I Council, acting on a recommendation from the NCAA Sports Oversight Committee, voted in June 2025 to separate men's and women's scoring in the fencing championship, which would effectively establish separate men's and women's championships in that sport. Because fencing is a National Collegiate sport with a single championship event for all divisions, Divisions II and III had to separately approve this change.[[89]](#cite_note-94) The other divisions approved this change the following month.[[90]](#cite_note-95)

In the 2027–28 school year, separate divisional championships will be established in two women's sports—Division II bowling and Division III wrestling.[[76]](#cite_note-4NewChamps-79)

The Football Bowl Subdivision of Division I determines its own champion separately from the NCAA via the [College Football Playoff](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff "College Football Playoff"); this is not an official NCAA championship (see below).

The NCAA awards championships in the sports listed below. For the two coeducational championships, women's dates reflect the first championship that was open to women.

### Emerging sports for women

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Emerging sports for women")]

Main article: [NCAA Emerging Sports for Women](/wiki/NCAA_Emerging_Sports_for_Women "NCAA Emerging Sports for Women")

In addition to the above sports, the NCAA recognizes Emerging Sports for Women. These sports have roster (Division I) and scholarship (Division II) limitations for each sport, but do not currently have officially sanctioned NCAA championships. A member institution may use these sports to meet the required level of sports sponsorship for its division. An "Emerging Sport" must gain championship status (minimum 40 varsity programs for team sports, except 28 for Division III) within 10 years, or show steady progress toward that goal to remain on the list.[[93]](#cite_note-103) Until then, it is under the auspices of the NCAA and its respective institutions. Emerging Sport status allows for competition to include club teams to satisfy the minimum number of competitions bylaw established by the NCAA.

The four sports currently designated as Emerging Sports for Women are:

* [Equestrian](/wiki/Equestrianism "Equestrianism") (in Divisions I and II only)
* [Flag football](/wiki/Flag_football "Flag football")
* [Rugby](/wiki/College_rugby "College rugby")
* [Triathlon](/wiki/Triathlon "Triathlon")

[Wrestling](/wiki/Freestyle_wrestling "Freestyle wrestling") was removed from the Emerging Sports program in January 2025 when it became an official championship sport, with the first season taking place in 2025–26. Similarly, [acrobatics](/wiki/Acrobatic_gymnastics "Acrobatic gymnastics") & [tumbling](/wiki/Tumbling_(sport) "Tumbling (sport)") and stunt were removed from the program in January 2026, with the first seasons to take place in 2026–27.

The newest Emerging Sport is flag football, added to the program in January 2026 with the first season under "emerging" status in spring 2026.[[76]](#cite_note-4NewChamps-79)

## Sports added and dropped

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Sports added and dropped")]

The popularity of each of these sports programs has changed over time. Between 1988–89 and 2010–11, NCAA schools had net additions of 510 men's teams and 2,703 women's teams.[[94]](#cite_note-ncaapublications.com-104)

The following tables show the changes over time in the number of NCAA schools across all three divisions combined sponsoring each of the men's and women's team sports.

### Men's sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Men's sports")]

The men's sports with the biggest net gains during the 1988–89 to 2010–11 period were indoor track and field, lacrosse, and cross country (each with more than 100 net gains). The men's sports with the biggest losses were wrestling (−104 teams), tennis, and rifle; the men's team sport with the most net losses was water polo.[[94]](#cite_note-ncaapublications.com-104) Other reports show that 355 college wrestling programs have been eliminated since 2000; 212 men's gymnastics programs have been eliminated since 1969 with only 17 programs remaining as of 2013.[[95]](#cite_note-105)

Additionally, eight NCAA sports—all men's sports—were sponsored by fewer Division I schools in 2020 than in 1990, despite the D-I membership having increased by nearly 60 schools during that period. Four of these sports, namely wrestling, swimming & diving, gymnastics, and tennis, lost more than 20 net teams during that timeframe. As a proportion of D-I membership, men's tennis took the greatest hit; 71.5% of D-I members had men's tennis in 2020, compared to 93.2% in 1990.[[96]](#cite_note-106)

### Trophies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Trophies")]

For every NCAA sanctioned sport other than Division I FBS football, the NCAA awards trophies with gold, silver, and bronze plating for the first-, second-, and third-place teams, respectively.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the case of the NCAA basketball tournaments, both semifinalists who did not make the championship game receive bronze plated trophies for third place (prior to 1982 the teams played a "consolation" game to determine third place).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Similar trophies are awarded to both semifinalists in the NCAA football tournaments (which are conducted in Division I FCS and both lower divisions), which have never had a third-place game. Winning teams maintain permanent possession of these trophies unless it is later found that they were won via serious rules violations.

Starting with the 2001–02 season, and again in the 2007–08 season, the trophies were changed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Starting in the 2006 basketball season, teams that make the Final Four in the Division I tournament receive bronze-plated "regional championship" trophies upon winning their Regional Championship which state the region they won and have the Final Four logo. The teams that make the National Championship game receive an additional trophy that is gold-plated for the winner. Starting in the mid-1990s, the National Champions in men's and women's basketball receive an elaborate trophy with a black marble base and crystal "neck" with a removable crystal basketball following the presentation of the standard NCAA Championship trophy.

As of May 30, 2022,[[99]](#cite_note-109) [Stanford](/wiki/Stanford_Cardinal "Stanford Cardinal"), [UCLA](/wiki/UCLA_Bruins "UCLA Bruins"), and [Southern California](/wiki/USC_Trojans "USC Trojans") (USC) have the most NCAA championships. Stanford has won 131 and UCLA has won 119 NCAA team championships in men's and women's sports, while USC is third with 111.

### Football Bowl Subdivision

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Football Bowl Subdivision")]

Main articles: [College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS](/wiki/College_football_national_championships_in_NCAA_Division_I_FBS "College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS") and [College Football Playoff](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff "College Football Playoff")

The NCAA has never sanctioned an official championship for its highest level of football, now known as [Division I FBS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision"). Instead, several outside bodies award their own titles. The NCAA does not hold a championship tournament or game for Division I FBS football. In the past, teams that placed first in any of a number of season-ending media polls, most notable the [AP Poll](/wiki/AP_Poll "AP Poll") of writers and the [Coaches Poll](/wiki/Coaches_Poll "Coaches Poll"), were said to have won the "[national championship](/wiki/Mythical_national_championship "Mythical national championship")".

From 2014 through 2023, the [College Football Playoff](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff "College Football Playoff") – a consortium of the conferences and [independent schools](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_FBS_independent_schools "NCAA Division I FBS independent schools") that compete in Division I FBS and six [bowl games](/wiki/Bowl_game "Bowl game") – has arranged to place the top four teams (based on a thirteen-member committee that selects and seeds the teams) into two semifinal games, with the winners advancing to compete in the [College Football Playoff National Championship](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff_National_Championship "College Football Playoff National Championship"), which is not officially sanctioned or recognized by the NCAA. The winner of the game receives a trophy; since the NCAA awards no national championship for Division I FBS football, this trophy does not denote *NCAA* as other NCAA college sports national championship trophies do. The playoff expanded to 12 teams starting in 2024.

## Conferences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Conferences")]

See also: [List of NCAA conferences](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_conferences "List of NCAA conferences") and [List of college athletic conferences in the United States](/wiki/List_of_college_athletic_conferences_in_the_United_States "List of college athletic conferences in the United States")

The NCAA is divided into three levels of conferences, [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I"), [Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II"), and [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III"), organized in declining program size, as well as numerous sub-divisions. Most schools belong to a primary "multisport conference" for most of their sports. Schools may belong to different conferences for different sports.

The Division I, Division II, and Division III "Independents" listed below are not conferences per se; it is a designation used for schools that do not belong to a conference for a particular sport. These schools may still have conference memberships for other sports. For example, [Notre Dame](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish "Notre Dame Fighting Irish") primarily belongs to the [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference") for most sports, but its ice hockey team competes in the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference") and its football team is an independent.

### Division I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Division I")]

Main articles: [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") and [List of NCAA Division I athletic directors](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_Division_I_athletic_directors "List of NCAA Division I athletic directors")

Among the NCAA regulations, each Division I conference defined as a "multisport conference" must have at least seven active Division I member institutions. These conferences must sponsor at least 12 sports, including six sports for men and six for women. At least seven active members in a multisport conference must sponsor both men's and women's basketball. However, a conference may operate for up to 2 years with fewer active members under a hardship rule. For non-football conferences, they must sponsor at least two men's team sports other than basketball. Teams that consist of both men and women are counted as men's teams for sports sponsorship purposes.[[100]](#cite_note-D-I_multisport_conferences-111)

For all institutions in the Division I Football Bowl Subdivision, they have additional requirements. Among them, they must participate in conference play in at least six men's and eight women's sports, including football, men's and women's basketball, and at least two other women's team sports.[[101]](#cite_note-112)[[102]](#cite_note-NCAA_Division_Differences-113) In 2023, the NCAA added new requirements for FBS membership, set to take effect in 2027–28. At that time, FBS institutions must fund the equivalent of 210 full scholarships across all of their NCAA sports; must spend at least $6 million annually on said scholarships; and must provide at least 90% of the required number of full scholarships across 16 sports (as chosen by the institution), including football.[[103]](#cite_note-114)
